blurb: A lemon-scented annual bee balm whose stems stack tier upon tier of tubular pink-to-lavender flowers between showy bracts, like a pagoda. Easy from seed, it self-sows into colonies and draws clouds of bees, butterflies and hummingbirds.
water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
spread: Upright annual; self-sows freely into colonies
seasons: Lemon-scented foliage rosette through spring; tiered pink-lavender-white flower whorls early–midsummer; tan tiered seed spikes after
wildlife: Magnet for bees, butterflies and hummingbirds; a top nectar annual
origin: South-central US (TX, OK, KS, NM) & N Mexico
habitat: Prairies, glades & open ground on limestone

care.hardiness: Grown as a warm-season annual here — it completes its cycle in one season and is killed by frost, so winter-hardiness is moot; it reliably self-sows where it's happy, returning from dropped seed each spring.[1][2]
care.planting: Direct-sow or transplant out in spring after the mid-May average last frost into average, well-drained ground; it also takes a fall sowing that germinates the next spring. In containers use a free-draining mix and full sun — it makes an easy, airy pot annual; no overwintering is needed since the plant dies at frost (just let a few seedheads ripen to reseed).[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun (6+ hours) for the fullest, most colorful tiered spikes; it tolerates light afternoon shade but blooms thinner in deeper shade.[1][2]
care.soil: Easy and unfussy — prefers rocky limestone or sandy-loam soil but adapts to most well-drained ground, including Boulder's leaner alkaline soils (about pH 6.0–7.8). Good drainage matters more than fertility; it dislikes soggy ground.[1][2]
care.water: Low to moderate and drought-tolerant once up — water to establish seedlings, then only in extended heat; tolerant of occasionally dry soil, which suits our semi-arid summers.[1][2]
care.spacing: Thin or space plants about 10–12 in apart — slender uprights that don't need much room — keeping the stand open for airflow against mildew.[1][2]
care.propagation: Almost always grown from seed: surface-sow (the seed wants light) in fall or early spring, and it germinates readily; once established it self-seeds freely into colonies. Let a few spent spikes ripen and scatter to keep it coming back.[1][2]
care.bloom: Tiers of tubular two-lipped lavender-to-pink (sometimes white) flowers stacked between purple-tinged bracts, spring into midsummer — roughly June–July here; deadheading prolongs it, but leave some heads to self-sow.[1][2]
care.feeding: Little to none — average soil is plenty; rich feeding makes soft, lanky, mildew-prone growth.[2]
care.maintenance: Very low: an annual that needs only thinning for airflow and optional deadheading. Pull or thin volunteer seedlings in spring to keep the colony in bounds; clear frost-killed stems in fall, leaving a few seedheads to reseed.[1][2]
care.selfsow: Self-sows freely and forms colonies where happy; deadhead before seed-set or thin the spring volunteers to control its spread, and site it where a drift is welcome.[1][2]
care.troubles: Powdery mildew is the genus's signature problem, worst in shade, crowding or still air — give it full sun, lean well-drained soil and open spacing to prevent it; otherwise nearly trouble-free and deer/rabbit resistant.[1][2]
care.companions: A long-blooming nectar annual for sunny pollinator borders, meadows and cottage plantings — pair with other prairie annuals, salvias and grasses.[1][2]

references.name: NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Monarda citriodora
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Monarda citriodora (Lemon beebalm)
USDA NRCS PLANTS Database — Monarda citriodora

edible.summary: A lemon-scented mint-family herb — the citrusy leaves and flowers make a refreshing tea and a flavoring for salads and cooked dishes.[1][2]
edible.parts: The aromatic leaves and flowers are the edible parts, prized for a distinct lemon-mint scent and flavor.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Use leaves and flowers raw or cooked — steep them fresh or dried for a lemony tea, scatter the flowers over salads as an edible garnish, or chop the leaves to season cooked foods. Harvest at peak bloom when the oils are strongest.[1][2]
edible.caution: A recognized culinary aromatic with no notable toxicity, but it is strongly flavored — use as a seasoning/tea rather than a bulk food, and confirm the lemon-scented identity before eating. Those sensitive to mint-family herbs should sample cautiously.[1][2]
